expanded_description: An introduction to Phylum Chordata—the animal group that includes all vertebrates (fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, mammals) plus two groups of invertebrate chordates. This guide explains the four features that define chordates, how these features appear and change across different chordate groups, and what this tells us about evolution.

core_concept.principle: All chordates share four features at some point in their life: a notochord (flexible rod for support), a dorsal hollow nerve cord (becomes the brain and spinal cord), pharyngeal slits (openings in the throat), and a post-anal tail (extends beyond the anus). These features may appear only in embryos or be modified in adults.
core_concept.explanation:
Phylum Chordata includes about 65,000 species, from tiny filter-feeders to whales and humans. What makes an animal a chordate? Four key features appear at some stage of life:

1. Notochord: A flexible rod that runs along the back. It provides support and gives muscles something to pull against. In vertebrates, it is replaced by the backbone (vertebral column) during development, but it remains as the soft center of spinal discs.

2. Dorsal hollow nerve cord: A tube of nerve tissue that runs along the back, above the notochord. In vertebrates, it develops into the brain and spinal cord. It is "hollow" because it forms by folding of tissue during development—unlike the solid nerve cords of worms or insects.

3. Pharyngeal slits: Openings in the throat (pharynx). In filter-feeding chordates, they help trap food. In fish, they become gills for breathing. In land animals, they appear only in embryos and later become parts of the ears, jaws, and throat.

4. Post-anal tail: A tail that extends beyond the anus. In aquatic chordates, it helps with swimming. In many land animals, it is reduced or lost. Humans have a tailbone (coccyx) as a remnant.

These features may be present throughout life (as in lancelets), only in larvae (as in tunicates), or only in embryos (as in humans). This pattern reveals evolutionary history—we share these features because we inherited them from a common ancestor.

Fun fact: Human embryos have all four chordate features. At about 4-5 weeks, a human embryo has a notochord, a hollow nerve cord, pharyngeal slits (which become parts of the ear and throat), and a tail (which later disappears). This is a clue to our evolutionary past.

image.alt: Diagram showing the four chordate features: notochord (flexible rod), dorsal hollow nerve cord (tube above it), pharyngeal slits (openings in throat), post-anal tail (extends beyond anus).
image.caption: Four chordate features: Notochord (support), dorsal hollow nerve cord (central nervous system), pharyngeal slits (filter-feeding/gills), post-anal tail (locomotion). All chordates have these at some life stage.

sections.introduction:
If you look at a fish, a bird, a lizard, and a human, they don't look much alike. But beneath the surface, they share a common body plan that goes back over 500 million years. All of them are chordates—members of the phylum Chordata.

Chordates include all vertebrates (animals with backbones) plus two groups of invertebrate chordates that lack backbones: lancelets and tunicates. There are about 65,000 chordate species, and vertebrates make up most of them (about 62,000 species).

What makes a chordate? Four key features appear at some stage of life:
• A notochord—a flexible rod that supports the body
• A dorsal hollow nerve cord—the beginning of the central nervous system
• Pharyngeal slits—openings in the throat
• A post-anal tail—a tail that extends beyond the anus

Not all chordates have these features as adults. Some keep them their whole lives; others have them only as embryos or larvae. This pattern tells us about evolution—features that appear in embryos of modern animals were present in their ancient ancestors.

In this section, we'll explore each of the four chordate features, how they appear in different chordate groups, and what this reveals about our own evolutionary history.

introduction_image.alt: Three chordate groups: Lancelet (small fish-like filter-feeder), Tunicate (sessile adult, swimming larva), Vertebrate (fish, amphibian, reptile, bird, mammal).
introduction_image.caption: Chordate groups: Lancelets (invertebrate chordates, retain all four features), Tunicates (invertebrate chordates, larvae have features, adults lose most), Vertebrates (backbone, modify features as adults).

key_concept.definition: Chordates are animals that have, at some life stage, a notochord (flexible rod), a dorsal hollow nerve cord, pharyngeal slits, and a post-anal tail. These features may be present throughout life, only in larvae, or only in embryos.
key_concept.context:
The four chordate features tell us about evolutionary relationships. Lancelets keep all four as adults—they show us what early chordates may have looked like. Tunicate larvae have all four but lose most as adults—this shows that chordate features can be lost in some lineages. Vertebrates have all four as embryos but modify them as adults—the notochord becomes the backbone, the nerve cord becomes the brain and spinal cord, the pharyngeal slits become gills or ear/jaw structures, and the tail may reduce or disappear.

This pattern of "appearing in embryos, modified in adults" is evidence of evolution. Our embryos still show features we inherited from distant ancestors, even if we no longer need them as adults.

image.alt: Table comparing chordate features across lancelets, tunicates, and vertebrates.
image.caption: Chordate features across groups: Lancelets (all four as adults), Tunicates (all four as larvae, lost in adults), Vertebrates (all four as embryos, modified in adults).

sections.key_concept: The four chordate features—notochord, dorsal hollow nerve cord, pharyngeal slits, and post-anal tail—appear in different ways across chordate groups, revealing evolutionary relationships.

classifications.value: Understanding the rod that supports the body and gives rise to the backbone
characteristics: Structure: A flexible rod of cells running from head to tail. It is stiff but bendable, like a plastic drinking straw.
Position: Located just below the nerve cord and above the digestive tract.
Function: Provides support for the body and gives muscles something to attach to.
In lancelets: Present throughout life as the main support structure.
In tunicates: Present only in the larva's tail (helps swimming), lost in adults.
In vertebrates: Present in embryos, replaced by the backbone (vertebral column) as the animal grows. The soft center of spinal discs (nucleus pulposus) is a remnant of the notochord.
classifications.behavior:
The notochord is the original "backbone." It gives the body stiffness while still allowing bending. Muscles attach to it, so when muscles contract, the body bends from side to side—this is how early chordates swam.

When vertebrates evolved, they developed vertebrae (backbones) that surrounded the notochord. The vertebrae provided better protection for the nerve cord and allowed for larger body size. The notochord didn't disappear entirely—it remains as the jelly-like center of the discs between your vertebrae.

Every time you bend your back, you're using a structure that evolved from the notochord. When a disc "slips" (herniates), the jelly-like center pushes out—that's your notochord remnant causing trouble!

Lancelets show us what the early chordate body plan looked like: a notochord running the whole length, with muscles attached to it, swimming by bending side to side. This is the basic design that vertebrates later built upon.

examples.explanation:
Lancelets are small (about 5 cm long), fish-like animals that live buried in sand. When disturbed, they swim by bending their bodies side to side.
(1) How it works: Muscles attach to the notochord. When muscles on one side contract, the body bends that way. Alternating contractions create waves that push the animal forward.
(2) Why it matters: This is how early chordates swam. It's the same basic movement fish use today—but fish have vertebrae around their notochord, making them stronger and able to grow larger.
(3) What it tells us: Lancelets are living models of what the earliest chordates may have looked like—simple, filter-feeding animals with a notochord for support and muscles for swimming.

image.alt: Lancelet anatomy showing notochord, muscle blocks, and undulatory swimming motion.
image.caption: Lancelet swimming: Muscles attach to notochord → alternating contractions → body bends side to side → forward movement. This is the basic chordate swimming method.

examples.explanation:
In vertebrate embryos, the notochord forms first. It tells the body which side is which and signals the nerve cord to form. Then, vertebrae grow around it.
(1) Embryo stage: Notochord runs the length of the body. Cells that will become vertebrae migrate to surround it.
(2) Growing stage: Vertebrae form around the notochord, like rings around a rod.
(3) Adult: The notochord mostly disappears, but its remnant remains as the soft center (nucleus pulposus) of each spinal disc.

Why does this matter? This is a clear example of evolution modifying an existing structure. The notochord was useful for support and swimming. Vertebrates built upon it, adding vertebrae for protection and strength. They didn't throw away the notochord—they kept part of it as shock absorbers between vertebrae.

image.alt: Development: Embryo notochord → vertebrae form around it → adult with backbone and disc remnants.
image.caption: Notochord to backbone: Embryonic notochord → vertebrae form around it → adult has backbone; notochord remains as disc centers (nucleus pulposus).

classifications.type: Dorsal Hollow Nerve Cord: The Nervous System
classifications.value: Understanding how the central nervous system develops and evolved
characteristics: Structure: A hollow tube of nerve tissue running along the back, above the notochord.
Formation: Develops from a groove in the embryo's skin that folds into a tube.
Function: Processes information and coordinates movement.
In lancelets: A simple tube with a slight swelling at the front (simple "brain").
In tunicates: Present in larvae (helps swimming), lost in adults.
In vertebrates: The front end enlarges into a complex brain; the rest becomes the spinal cord.
classifications.behavior:
The dorsal hollow nerve cord is a defining feature of chordates. It's different from the nerve cords of worms or insects, which are solid and located on the underside of the body.

How it forms: In the embryo, a strip of skin (the neural plate) folds into a groove, then the edges meet and seal, forming a tube. This tube becomes the brain and spinal cord.

Why "hollow" matters: The hollow space inside becomes the central canal of the spinal cord and the fluid-filled ventricles of the brain. This may help with circulation of nutrients and waste products.

The front end of the nerve cord has enlarged in vertebrates to become a complex brain. The brain controls everything from basic reflexes to complex thought. The size and complexity of the brain varies across vertebrates—from the simple brain of a fish to the highly developed brain of a human.

Lancelets have a simple nerve cord with a small swelling at the front—a very basic "brain." This gives us a glimpse of what the earliest chordate nervous system looked like before brains became complex.

examples.explanation:
The nerve cord forms in the embryo through a process called neurulation:
(1) Neural plate: A flat sheet of cells on the back of the embryo, above the notochord.
(2) Neural groove: The edges of the plate rise up, forming a groove down the middle.
(3) Neural tube: The edges meet and fuse, creating a hollow tube.
(4) Brain and spinal cord: The front end enlarges; the rest stays tubular.

What happens if this goes wrong? In humans, failure of the neural tube to close properly causes birth defects like spina bifida (incomplete closing of the spinal cord) or anencephaly (incomplete brain development). This is why folic acid is recommended during pregnancy—it helps prevent neural tube defects.

Why does the nerve cord form this way? This process is ancient—it happens in all chordates, from lancelets to humans. It's evidence that all chordates share a common ancestor that used this method to build its nervous system.

image.alt: Stages: Neural plate → groove → tube → brain and spinal cord.
image.caption: Nerve cord formation: Neural plate (flat) → Neural groove (folded) → Neural tube (hollow) → Brain and spinal cord. Same process in all chordates.

examples.explanation:
The vertebrate brain has three main parts, all derived from the front end of the nerve cord:
• Forebrain: Smell, vision, complex thought (in mammals)
• Midbrain: Vision and hearing reflexes
• Hindbrain: Balance, basic body functions (breathing, heart rate)

In fish, the brain is relatively simple—most of it is devoted to smell and vision. In amphibians and reptiles, the brain is more complex. In mammals, the forebrain has expanded enormously, especially the cerebral cortex (responsible for thinking, memory, language).

Lancelets have only a slight swelling at the front of the nerve cord—no distinct brain regions. This shows us what the earliest chordate brain looked like. Over millions of years, the front end of the nerve cord expanded and specialized, giving rise to the complex brains we see in vertebrates today.

image.alt: Brain evolution from simple nerve cord swelling (lancelet) to fish, reptile, mammal with enlarged forebrain.
image.caption: Brain evolution: Lancelet (simple swelling) → Fish (basic regions) → Reptile (more complex) → Mammal (large forebrain). All from the same basic nerve cord.

classifications.type: Pharyngeal Slits: Openings in the Throat
classifications.value: Understanding how feeding and breathing structures evolved
characteristics: Structure: Openings in the throat (pharynx) that connect to the outside.
Ancestral function: Filter-feeding—water enters the mouth, food is trapped, water exits through slits.
In fish: Become gills for breathing—water flows over gill filaments, oxygen is absorbed.
In land animals: Appear only in embryos; later become parts of the ear, jaw, and throat.
In humans: Embryonic slits close; parts become the eustachian tube (connects ear to throat), tonsils, and structures in the neck.
classifications.behavior:
Pharyngeal slits are the most adaptable of the chordate features. In different groups, they do completely different jobs.

In lancelets and tunicate larvae, the slits are for feeding. Water flows in the mouth, through the slits, and out. Food particles get trapped in mucus and are moved to the gut. This is called filter-feeding.

In fish, the slits became gills. The tissue between the slits forms arches that support gill filaments. Blood vessels in the gills absorb oxygen from water and release carbon dioxide. Fish have 5-7 pairs of gill slits.

In land animals (amphibians, reptiles, birds, mammals), the slits appear only in embryos. They close as development proceeds, but the arches between them give rise to important structures:
• First arch: Becomes the jaws
• Second arch: Becomes parts of the ear and throat
• Other arches: Become parts of the larynx (voice box) and throat muscles

This explains why the ear, jaw, and throat are connected—they all develop from the same embryonic structures. It also explains why some birth defects affect multiple areas (cleft palate, ear malformations, jaw problems) together.

examples.process: From Fish Gills to Human Ears
examples.explanation:
The same embryonic structures that become gills in fish become parts of the ear and jaw in humans:
(1) First pharyngeal arch: Becomes the jaws and two ear bones (malleus and incus).
(2) Second pharyngeal arch: Becomes the third ear bone (stapes) and part of the throat (hyoid bone).
(3) Remaining arches: Become parts of the larynx (voice box) and throat muscles.

Why does this matter? It explains why ear infections are connected to the throat—the eustachian tube (from the first pharyngeal slit) connects the middle ear to the throat. It also explains why jaw problems can affect hearing—they share the same developmental origin.

This is also evidence of evolution. In fish, these structures are gills. In land animals, they are repurposed for breathing air (larynx) and hearing (ear bones). The bones in your ear were once part of a fish's jaw. Fossils of transitional animals show intermediate stages—jaw bones becoming ear bones over millions of years.

image.alt: Fish gills vs. human ear/jaw structures, showing same embryonic origin.
image.caption: Pharyngeal arches: Fish gills (breathing) → Human ear and jaw structures. Same embryonic origin, different adult functions. The bones in your ear were once part of a fish's jaw.

examples.explanation:
At about 4 weeks, human embryos have visible pharyngeal slits (often called "gill slits" in textbooks). They look remarkably like fish gill slits.
(1) Week 4: Four to six pairs of slits appear in the neck region.
(2) Weeks 5-8: The slits close; the arches between them develop into various structures.
(3) Adult: The first slit becomes the eustachian tube (connects ear to throat). The second slit becomes the tonsils. The remaining slits close completely.

Why do human embryos have "gill slits"? We don't need them—we don't breathe water. But we have them because we inherited the developmental program from fish ancestors. It's easier for evolution to modify an existing program than to create a new one from scratch.

This is a powerful piece of evidence for evolution. All vertebrate embryos—fish, reptiles, birds, mammals—go through similar stages. The differences come later. This is why embryos are so useful for understanding evolutionary relationships.

image.alt: Human embryo at 4 weeks showing pharyngeal slits, compared to fish embryo.
image.caption: Human embryo (4 weeks) has pharyngeal slits like a fish embryo. They close and become ear, jaw, and throat structures. This reflects our shared evolutionary history.

classifications.type: Post-Anal Tail: The Tail Beyond the Anus
classifications.value: Understanding the tail and how it changed during evolution
characteristics: Structure: A muscular extension of the body beyond the anus.
Ancestral function: Swimming—side-to-side undulation pushes through water.
In fish: Tail is large and powerful with a fin for propulsion.
In land animals: Tail may be used for balance (kangaroos, dinosaurs), communication (dogs), grasping (monkeys), or reduced/absent (humans, apes).
In humans: Embryonic tail present (weeks 4-8); later reduced to the coccyx (tailbone).
classifications.behavior:
The post-anal tail is what makes a chordate tail different from other animals' tails. In most animals, the anus is at the end of the body. In chordates, the body extends beyond the anus, forming a tail.

In aquatic chordates (fish, whales, dolphins), the tail is the main swimming organ. It moves side to side, pushing against water to propel the animal forward. Even land animals that return to water (whales, dolphins, manatees) still use a tail—though theirs moves up and down, not side to side.

In land animals, the tail has taken on many jobs:
• Balance: Kangaroos use their tail as a fifth limb when hopping slowly. Dinosaurs used their tails for counterbalance.
• Communication: Dogs wag tails, deer raise white tails as warning signals.
• Grasping: Monkeys with prehensile tails can hold onto branches.

In apes and humans, the tail is greatly reduced. Our ancestors lost the need for a tail when they started walking upright. But the tail doesn't disappear completely—it becomes the coccyx (tailbone), a small set of fused vertebrae at the bottom of the spine. The coccyx is not useless—it anchors muscles of the pelvic floor, which are important for controlling the bladder and bowel and for childbirth.

Human embryos develop a tail around 4-5 weeks. It has 10-12 vertebrae. Later, most of it disappears through programmed cell death (apoptosis), leaving only the coccyx. Occasionally, babies are born with small tails (usually 1-2 cm, no vertebrae) that are surgically removed—a harmless reminder of our tailed ancestors.

examples.explanation:
Human embryos go through a "tailed" stage:
(1) Week 4: Tail bud forms; extends beyond the developing legs.
(2) Weeks 5-6: Tail contains 10-12 developing vertebrae.
(3) Weeks 7-8: Programmed cell death (apoptosis) reduces the tail; vertebrae fuse.
(4) Adult: Coccyx (tailbone) remains—4 fused vertebrae.

Why do human embryos have a tail? Because our ancestors had tails. The developmental program for making a tail is still in our DNA, but it's mostly turned off. It's easier for evolution to suppress a structure (by adding a "stop" signal) than to remove it entirely from the developmental program.

Rare cases: About 100 cases of babies born with tails have been documented. These tails contain fat, connective tissue, muscle, and nerves but no vertebrae. They are surgically removed and cause no long-term problems. They are a reminder that the genetic instructions for a tail are still present in humans, even if they're usually turned off.

image.alt: Human embryo tail (week 5) and adult coccyx.
image.caption: Human embryo has a tail (weeks 4-8) with 10-12 vertebrae. It later reduces to the coccyx (tailbone). This reflects our evolutionary history.

examples.explanation:
Vertebrate tails show amazing diversity based on what the animal needs:
• Swimming: Fish tails provide propulsion. Whale tails (flukes) have evolved for powerful swimming, though they move up and down.
• Balance: Kangaroos use their tail as a fifth leg. Cheetahs use their tail for balance during high-speed turns.
• Communication: Dogs express emotion through tail position. Beavers slap tails as warnings.
• Grasping: Some monkeys have prehensile tails that can hold branches, acting as a fifth hand.
• Reduced: Apes and humans have greatly reduced tails. Frogs lose their tails during metamorphosis.

This diversity shows evolution in action. The same basic structure (a tail with vertebrae and muscles) has been modified countless times to serve different functions. What started as a simple swimming organ has become a balance beam, a communication tool, a grasping organ, or a nearly invisible remnant—all depending on what the animal needed.

image.alt: Fish (swimming), kangaroo (balance), monkey (grasping), human (coccyx).
image.caption: Tail diversity: Swimming (fish), balance (kangaroo), grasping (monkey), reduced (human). Same basic structure, modified for different needs.

principles.statement: The presence, modification, or loss of chordate features in different groups shows how they are related. Lancelets keep all four features as adults—they show us what early chordates may have looked like. Tunicates have them as larvae but lose them as adults—showing features can be lost in some lineages. Vertebrates have them as embryos but modify them as adults—showing how evolution builds on existing structures.
principles.application:
This helps us understand:
• Why all vertebrate embryos look similar (they all follow the same basic plan)
• Why we have structures we don't seem to need (like embryonic tails and pharyngeal slits)
• How to interpret fossils—if an ancient animal has any of these features, it's a chordate, even if it lacks a backbone

**Everyday connection**: When you hear about a fossil being called a "primitive chordate," it means it has some of these features but not a full backbone. These fossils help us understand how vertebrates evolved.
principles.example: The fossil Haikouella (found in China, about 530 million years old) has a notochord, muscle blocks, and a dorsal nerve cord—but no vertebrae or skull. It's clearly a chordate, but not yet a vertebrate. It shows us what the ancestors of vertebrates looked like before they evolved backbones.
principles.analogy: Chordate features are like family traits. If your cousin has red hair and freckles (like you), you share a common ancestor. If you have red hair but your cousin doesn't, the trait was lost in their branch. The same with chordate features—some groups keep them, some lose them, but their presence tells us about shared ancestry.

image.alt: Evolutionary tree showing lancelets, tunicates, and vertebrates as branches from common ancestor.
image.caption: Chordate relationships: Lancelets (keep all features), Tunicates (lose features as adults), Vertebrates (modify features). All share a common ancestor that had the four features.

principles.principle: Vertebrate Innovations Were Built on Chordate Foundations
principles.statement: Vertebrates didn't start from scratch. They took the chordate body plan and added new features: vertebrae to protect the nerve cord, a skull to protect the brain, jaws to eat larger prey, and paired fins (later legs) for better movement. Each innovation was built on structures that were already there.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• Vertebrae form around the notochord (not instead of it)
• Jaws develop from the first pharyngeal arch (not from new tissue)
• Limbs develop from fins (not as new growths)

**Everyday connection**: Your backbone formed around your notochord. Your jaw developed from the same structures that were gill supports in fish. Your limbs are modified fins. Nothing is completely new—everything is an upgrade.
principles.example: Jaw evolution: The earliest fish (like ostracoderms) had no jaws. They filter-fed or sucked up food. Later fish (like placoderms) evolved jaws from the first pharyngeal arch. The bones in your jaw and ear are modified from those ancient fish structures. Fossils show intermediate stages—arch bones gradually changed shape to become jaws.
principles.analogy: Vertebrate evolution is like renovating a house. The foundation (chordate body plan) stays. You add rooms (backbone, skull). You remodel existing rooms (pharyngeal arches become jaws). You add new features (paired limbs). The original foundation is still there, but the house is much more complex.

image.alt: Diagram: Notochord (original) → vertebrae added; pharyngeal arches → jaws; fins → limbs.
image.caption: Vertebrate innovations: Backbone (added around notochord), Jaws (from pharyngeal arches), Limbs (from fins). Built on chordate foundation, not replacing it.

principles.principle: Living Invertebrate Chordates Are Models, Not Ancestors
principles.statement: Lancelets and tunicates are not our ancestors—they are modern animals that have been evolving for just as long as vertebrates. But they have kept features that vertebrates lost or modified, so they help us understand what the common ancestor of all chordates might have looked like.
principles.application:
This helps us avoid the mistake of thinking lancelets are "primitive" or "unchanged." They are just as evolved as we are—they just took a different path. Studying them helps us infer what ancient chordates were like because they retained ancestral features.

**Everyday connection**: If you want to know what a Model T Ford looked like, you don't need a time machine—you can look at one in a museum. Lancelets and tunicates are like museum specimens—they show us what ancient chordates may have looked like, even though they are modern animals.
principles.example: Lancelets have genes for vertebrate features (like parts of the immune system) that they don't actually use. The genes are there, but they're not activated. This tells us that the genetic toolkit for complex features existed before the features themselves. Vertebrates didn't invent new genes—they just found new ways to use old ones.
principles.analogy: Invertebrate chordates are like old photographs. They're not your actual ancestors, but they show you what your ancestors might have looked like. They've changed over time, but they kept features that your branch lost.

image.alt: Lancelet (modern) and Haikouella fossil (ancient), showing similar body plan.
image.caption: Lancelets are living models of early chordates. They are not ancestors (they've evolved for 500 million years too), but they show us what early chordates may have looked like.

components.definition: Chordates share four defining features: notochord (support), dorsal hollow nerve cord (nervous system), pharyngeal slits (filter-feeding/gills/ear structures), and post-anal tail (locomotion/balance). These are modified differently in each subphylum.
components.equation: Chordate diversity = how much features are modified × how many new features added
components.interpretation: Lancelets (minimal modification—keep all four as adults). Tunicates (extreme modification—larvae have all four, adults lose most). Vertebrates (moderate modification—embryos have all four, adults modify them and add new features like backbone, skull, jaws, limbs).

image.alt: Summary diagram of four chordate features with examples.
image.caption: Chordate features: Notochord (support), dorsal nerve cord (nervous system), pharyngeal slits (feeding/breathing), post-anal tail (movement). Modified differently across groups.

categories.description: Lancelets (Branchiostoma) are small, fish-like invertebrate chordates that live in shallow marine sand. They keep all four chordate features as adults, making them important models for understanding the ancestral chordate body plan.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Lancelet anatomy: About 5 cm long, transparent, burrows in sand with tail sticking out. Notochord runs whole length, persists throughout life. Nerve cord is simple tube with slight swelling at front (cerebral vesicle). Pharyngeal slits (50-100+ pairs) used for filter-feeding. Post-anal tail used for swimming when disturbed. Muscles (myomeres) are arranged in V-shaped blocks, like fish.

examples.explanation:
Lancelets are filter-feeders. They burrow in sand, leaving only their mouth exposed. Water flows in, food particles (algae, bacteria) are trapped in mucus on the pharyngeal slits, and the mucus is moved to the gut. Water exits through the slits and out an opening called the atriopore.
(1) Water enters mouth
(2) Pharyngeal slits filter food particles
(3) Food moves to gut
(4) Water exits through atriopore

This is how early chordates likely fed before some evolved into predators. It's still how lancelets and tunicate adults feed today.

image.alt: Lancelet filter-feeding: water in, food trapped on slits, water out.
image.caption: Lancelet feeding: Water enters mouth → food trapped on pharyngeal slits → food to gut → water exits. Ancestral chordate feeding method.

categories.description: Tunicates (sea squirts) are invertebrate chordates that show dramatic transformation between larval and adult stages. The free-swimming larva has all four chordate features; the sessile adult loses most of them and becomes a filter-feeding sac.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Tunicate life cycle:
• Larva: "Tadpole larva" swims using tail with notochord. Has dorsal nerve cord, pharyngeal slits, post-anal tail. Looks like a tiny tadpole.
• Settlement: After hours or days, larva attaches head-first to a rock or other surface.
• Metamorphosis: Tail resorbs (notochord, nerve cord, tail muscles disappear). Body rotates 90°. Pharynx enlarges.
• Adult: Sessile sac attached to surface. Two siphons (incurrent and excurrent) draw water in and out. Only pharyngeal slits remain from chordate features. Covered by "tunic" (cellulose-containing outer layer—unique among animals).

examples.explanation:
Tunicate metamorphosis is one of the most dramatic transformations in the animal kingdom:
(1) Larva: Free-swimming with notochord, nerve cord, tail, pharyngeal slits. Looks like a tiny tadpole.
(2) Settlement: Finds a suitable surface and attaches head-first.
(3) Metamorphosis: Tail resorbs; notochord, nerve cord, and tail muscles disappear. Body rotates so siphons point up.
(4) Adult: Sessile filter-feeding sac. Only pharyngeal slits remain.

Why does this matter? It shows that chordate features can be lost in some lineages. The tunicate ancestor likely kept all four features as adults, but the adult stage was modified for a sessile lifestyle. The larva still shows the ancestral chordate body plan. This is evidence that tunicates evolved from swimming ancestors.

image.alt: Tunicate life cycle: swimming larva (with all features) → attaches → metamorphosis → adult (sessile filter-feeder).
image.caption: Tunicate life cycle: Larva (all four chordate features) → attaches → metamorphosis → adult (only pharyngeal slits remain). Shows chordate features can be lost.

worked_examples.title: Case Study: What Makes a Chordate?
worked_examples.problem:
You are examining three specimens: (A) An adult lancelet, (B) A tunicate larva, (C) A 5-week human embryo.
(1) Which of the four chordate features does each have?
(2) Which features persist to adulthood in each?
(3) What does each tell us about chordate evolution?

step_by_step.calculation:
(A) Adult lancelet: All four features present—notochord (whole length), dorsal nerve cord, pharyngeal slits (many pairs), post-anal tail.
(B) Tunicate larva: All four features present—notochord in tail, dorsal nerve cord, pharyngeal slits, tail.
(C) Human embryo (week 5): All four features present—notochord (temporary), neural tube (nerve cord), pharyngeal slits (4-6 pairs), tail (10-12 vertebrae).

(A) Lancelet: All four persist (retains ancestral condition).
(B) Tunicate: Only pharyngeal slits persist (others lost during metamorphosis).
(C) Human: Notochord becomes discs (remnant), nerve cord becomes brain/spinal cord, slits become ear/jaw/throat structures, tail becomes coccyx.

(A) Lancelet: Shows what early chordates may have looked like—simple filter-feeder with notochord support.
(B) Tunicate larva: Shows chordate features can be lost in adults; larval form reveals ancestry.
(C) Human embryo: Shows all chordates share same developmental program; features are modified, not lost.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) All three specimens have all four chordate features at the stage examined. (2) Adult persistence: Lancelet (all four), Tunicate (only slits), Human (all four modified). (3) Lancelet shows ancestral condition; Tunicate shows features can be lost; Human embryo shows features are modified but developmentally present. This demonstrates that all chordates share a common ancestor and that features can be retained, lost, or modified depending on the lineage.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Chordate features are best understood by looking at different life stages and different groups. Features that appear in embryos of land animals (like human pharyngeal slits and tails) reveal our shared ancestry with fish and other chordates.

sections.introduction: All chordate embryos develop through similar stages, with the four defining features appearing in a predictable order. This flow shows how these features form.

process_flow.description: The notochord forms first, running along the future back. It defines the body axis and signals other tissues to develop.
process_flow.mechanism: Cells from the mesoderm (middle germ layer) line up and form a rod. The notochord sends signals that tell the skin above it to become nerve tissue.

image.alt: Notochord forming as a rod in the embryo.
image.caption: Step 1: Notochord forms. This flexible rod defines the body axis and signals the nerve cord to develop.

process_flow.description: The skin above the notochord folds inward to form a hollow tube—the nerve cord.
process_flow.mechanism: Notochord signals cause skin cells to thicken, then fold, then seal, creating a tube. This tube will become the brain and spinal cord.

image.alt: Neural plate folding into a tube above notochord.
image.caption: Step 2: Nerve cord forms. Skin folds inward, creating a hollow tube above the notochord. This becomes the brain and spinal cord.

process_flow.description: Openings form in the throat (pharyngeal slits) and the body extends beyond the anus (post-anal tail).
process_flow.mechanism: Pharyngeal slits: Endoderm (gut lining) and ectoderm (skin) meet and form openings. Number varies by species. Post-anal tail: The body grows past the anus, forming a tail with muscles and sometimes vertebrae.
process_flow.result: All four chordate features present in embryo

image.alt: Embryo with pharyngeal slits and tail.
image.caption: Step 3: Pharyngeal slits and tail develop. All four chordate features are now present. Fate varies by species.

sections.definition: These are more advanced concepts about how scientists have used DNA and fossils to understand chordate evolution and how chordate embryos develop.

process_types.energy_profile: Comparing DNA sequences has helped scientists figure out which chordates are most closely related to each other. Surprisingly, DNA shows that tunicates (not lancelets) are the closest invertebrate relatives of vertebrates.
process_types.direction: Before DNA: Scientists thought lancelets were closest to vertebrates (both keep notochord as adults). After DNA: Tunicates share more genes with vertebrates than lancelets do. Tunicates + vertebrates = sister groups; lancelets branched off earlier.
process_types.function: DNA evidence helps us build more accurate evolutionary trees and understand which features are ancestral vs. derived.
goals: Understand that DNA evidence can change our understanding of relationships
Learn that tunicates are our closest invertebrate relatives
Appreciate that appearance can be misleading (tunicate adults don't look like chordates, but their DNA says they are)

primary_example.explanation:
Before DNA sequencing, scientists thought lancelets (which keep a notochord as adults) were the closest relatives to vertebrates. Tunicates, with their weird adult bodies, seemed more different.

DNA tells a different story: Tunicates share more genes with vertebrates than lancelets do. This means tunicates and vertebrates share a more recent common ancestor. Lancelets branched off earlier.

Why does this matter? It means the ancestor of vertebrates was probably more like a tunicate larva (which has all four chordate features) than like a lancelet. Vertebrates may have evolved by keeping the larval features into adulthood (a process called paedomorphosis) and then adding new features like vertebrae and a skull.

This is a great example of how science works: new evidence (DNA) can overturn old ideas, and we build better understanding based on the best available data.

image.alt: Evolutionary tree: Lancelets (first branch), Tunicates and Vertebrates (later branch).
image.caption: DNA evidence: Tunicates (not lancelets) are the closest invertebrate relatives of vertebrates. This changed earlier ideas based on appearance alone.

image.alt: Tunicate larva (swimming) and vertebrate (fish), showing similarities.
image.caption: Paedomorphosis hypothesis: Vertebrates may have evolved from a tunicate-like ancestor that kept larval features into adulthood. Vertebrates look more like tunicate larvae than like adult tunicates.

process_types.energy_profile: Fossils from the Cambrian period (about 530 million years ago) show the earliest chordates and the first vertebrates. These fossils help us understand when and how vertebrates evolved.
process_types.direction: Early Cambrian: First chordate fossils (Haikouella, similar to lancelets) → Mid-Cambrian: First vertebrates (Myllokunmingia, with primitive vertebrae) → Later: Jaws, limbs, and other features evolve.
process_types.function: Fossils provide a timeline for chordate evolution and show the order in which features appeared.
goals: Learn about the earliest chordate fossils
Understand that vertebrae evolved before jaws and limbs
Appreciate that fossils and DNA tell complementary stories

primary_example.explanation:
Myllokunmingia is a fossil from China, about 530 million years old. It's about 2.5 cm long and looks like a tiny fish.
What makes it a vertebrate? It has a notochord (like all chordates) plus primitive vertebrae (small pieces of cartilage around the notochord) and a skull (cranium). It also has eyes, a brain, and muscle blocks (myomeres).

What it doesn't have: Jaws (evolved later), paired fins (evolved later).

Why this matters: Myllokunmingia shows that the first vertebrates appeared very early in the Cambrian, soon after the first chordates. Vertebrates didn't appear all at once—they started with a backbone and skull, then later added jaws, then fins, then legs. The order of appearance matches what we see in embryos: notochord → vertebrae → jaws → limbs.

image.alt: Myllokunmingia fossil reconstruction, showing notochord, primitive vertebrae, brain, eyes.
image.caption: Myllokunmingia (530 MYA): Earliest known vertebrate. Had notochord, primitive vertebrae, skull, brain, eyes. No jaws or paired fins yet.

image.alt: Timeline: 530 MYA (notochord, nerve cord), 530 MYA (vertebrae, skull), 430 MYA (jaws), 370 MYA (limbs).
image.caption: Vertebrate evolution timeline: Notochord and nerve cord (early), vertebrae and skull (530 MYA), jaws (430 MYA), limbs (370 MYA). Features appeared in sequence, not all at once.

sections.relationship: DNA evidence helps us understand which chordates are most closely related; fossils help us understand when features appeared. Together, they tell the story of chordate evolution—how simple filter-feeders became the complex vertebrates we see today.

summary:
Phylum Chordata includes all animals with four specific features at some point in their life: a notochord (flexible rod for support), a dorsal hollow nerve cord (becomes the brain and spinal cord), pharyngeal slits (openings in the throat), and a post-anal tail (extends beyond the anus).

Chordates are divided into three subphyla:
• Cephalochordates (lancelets) are small, fish-like filter-feeders that keep all four features as adults. They show what early chordates may have looked like.
• Urochordates (tunicates or sea squirts) have all four features as swimming larvae, but lose most as adults when they attach to rocks and become filter-feeders.
• Vertebrates (fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, mammals) have all four features as embryos. As adults, they modify these features: the notochord is replaced by the backbone (vertebral column), the nerve cord becomes the brain and spinal cord, the pharyngeal slits become gills in fish or parts of the ear and throat in land animals, and the tail may be reduced (humans have a tailbone called the coccyx).

Chordates evolved about 530 million years ago during the Cambrian period. The earliest chordates were small, soft-bodied filter-feeders. Vertebrates evolved by adding new features—a backbone to protect the nerve cord, a skull to protect the brain, jaws for eating, and paired fins or legs for moving. Fossils like Myllokunmingia from China (530 million years old) show some of the earliest vertebrates with primitive backbones.

Today, there are about 65,000 chordate species. Most (about 62,000) are vertebrates. The remaining 3,000 are tunicates and lancelets—living reminders of our distant evolutionary past.
